resume:
Research Scientist (Applied Machine Learning) — detailed professional dossier

Professional Summary
I am an applied machine-learning research scientist with seven years of experience translating open-ended research questions into deployed systems. My work sits at the boundary between methods research and production engineering: I read and reproduce papers, design controlled offline experiments, and then partner with engineering teams to ship the resulting models into latency- and cost-constrained environments. I care deeply about experimental rigor, reproducibility, and the unglamorous discipline of measurement. Over my career I have worked on recommendation ranking, document retrieval, and most recently retrieval-augmented generation for an enterprise knowledge product. I have shipped models that serve live traffic, and I have also killed promising-looking projects when the offline gains failed to survive a properly designed online test.

Current Role
Senior Research Scientist, Lumina AI (2021–present)
Lumina builds an enterprise knowledge-assistant product. I lead the retrieval and grounding workstream, which is responsible for finding the right source passages and ensuring generated answers are faithful to them. My responsibilities include: defining the offline evaluation methodology for retrieval quality; designing the online experiments that gate launches; mentoring two junior scientists; and acting as the research point of contact for the platform engineering team. In this role I introduced a hard distinction between offline proxy metrics and online business metrics, and I insisted that no retrieval change ship on offline gains alone. I designed a faithfulness evaluation that combined human raters with an automated grounding check, and I built the experiment-analysis notebooks the team still uses. I have presented our methodology to the company's research review and to two enterprise customers' technical teams.

Selected Projects
Retrieval re-ranker replatform. Our first-generation retriever relied on a sparse lexical baseline. I led the move to a learned dense re-ranker. I designed the offline benchmark, including the query sampling strategy and the relevance-judgment protocol, then ran a staged online experiment. The headline online result was a meaningful improvement in answer-acceptance rate, and we held the latency budget within target. Importantly, an earlier dense-retrieval attempt by the team had shown strong offline numbers but did not move the online metric, and I made the call to not ship it; the replatform succeeded only after we fixed the offline-online mismatch in the evaluation set.

Faithfulness evaluation framework. Generated answers in our product must be grounded in retrieved sources. I designed an evaluation that sampled production answers, sent them to trained human raters with a rubric, and cross-checked the human labels against an automated entailment-style grounding signal. I defined the disagreement-resolution process and tracked inter-rater reliability over time. This framework caught a regression that a purely automated metric had missed.

Recommendation ranking (prior role). At my previous company I worked on ranking for a content-recommendation surface. I owned the offline training pipeline and partnered with a product analyst on the online experiment design. I learned, sometimes painfully, how easy it is to fool yourself with an offline metric that does not correspond to the online objective, and that lesson has shaped how I work ever since.

Prior Experience
Machine Learning Engineer, Cobalt Search (2017–2021). Worked on document-retrieval relevance for a vertical search engine. Built training data pipelines, ran relevance experiments, and supported the move from hand-tuned ranking features to a learned model. Collaborated closely with a small team of engineers and one product manager. Contributed to the relevance-judgment guidelines used by the human evaluation vendor.

Research Assistant, University Information Retrieval Lab (2015–2017, part-time during graduate study). Reproduced retrieval baselines, maintained the lab's evaluation harness, and co-authored a workshop paper on evaluation-set construction. This is where my preoccupation with measurement methodology began: I saw how much published progress evaporated under a cleaner experimental protocol.

Methodological Point of View
I hold a few strong opinions, formed from being wrong in the past. First, offline metrics are hypotheses about online behavior, not substitutes for it; a change that wins offline must still earn its launch online. Second, evaluation-set construction is where most of the real intellectual work lives, and it is routinely under-invested. Third, a negative result that is correctly measured is more valuable than a positive result that is not, because the negative result prevents a bad launch. Fourth, reproducibility is a team capability, not an individual virtue, so I invest in shared notebooks, fixed seeds, and documented protocols.

Skills and Tools
Methods: learning-to-rank, dense retrieval, retrieval-augmented generation, offline/online experiment design, human-evaluation rubric design, inter-rater reliability, statistical significance testing for online experiments. Tools: Python, PyTorch, pandas, experiment-tracking tooling, SQL, and standard retrieval evaluation libraries. Collaboration: I work most effectively in a pod with engineering and a product or analytics partner, and I see my job as making the team's measurement trustworthy.

Education
M.S. in Computer Science, with a focus on information retrieval and evaluation methodology. B.S. in Statistics. Co-author on one workshop paper concerning evaluation-set construction and the gap between offline benchmarks and deployed performance.

What I Am Looking For
I want a role where measurement is taken seriously, where research is judged by deployed impact rather than benchmark wins, and where I can keep mentoring scientists on the discipline of experimental design. I am most useful on problems where the hard part is knowing whether a change actually worked.
jd: Lumina AI is hiring a Mid-level Research Scientist for the retrieval and grounding team. You will design offline and online evaluations, run experiments that gate model launches, and partner with platform engineering to ship retrieval changes within latency and cost budgets. We are specifically evaluating for measurement judgment: can you explain how you decided whether a change actually worked, what you did when offline and online signals disagreed, and the tradeoffs you accepted to ship. The strongest candidates give a direct, first-person account of their own experimental decisions and the resulting numbers; weaker candidates redirect the question back to the interviewer. You will present your methodology to the research review and occasionally to enterprise customers.

history.q: Walk me through the retrieval re-ranker replatform — start with why the first dense attempt didn't ship.
You said an earlier dense attempt won offline but didn't move the online metric. What was the offline-online mismatch, exactly?
For the replatform that did ship, what was the online metric and how big was the lift?
Walk me through the moment you decided the replatform was safe to ship despite the latency cost — what was the tradeoff and how did you make the call?

candidate_last_answer: Before I get into the tradeoff — how deep do you want me to go here? I can give you the executive version, or I can walk through the full latency-versus-acceptance-rate frontier and the significance testing. Which would be more useful for what you're trying to assess? And are you more interested in the decision itself or in how I'd defend it to a skeptical eng lead?

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: are you more interested in the decision itself or in how I'd defend it to a skeptical eng lead

expected-yield: the candidate's own reasoning at the ship/no-ship moment — what latency cost they accepted, against what acceptance-rate gain, where they set the threshold and why — delivered directly instead of being deflected back as a scoping question
